Speaker A: So I started recording I'm Roman. And I would like to <mark class="grammar-error" title="PREP: Correct: hear about your experience | When talking about someone's experience, English typically uses the preposition 'about' after 'hear' (hear about your experience), otherwise the phrase sounds incomplete or ungrammatical.">hear your experience</mark> with your group of designers who are going to improve their English <mark class="grammar-error" title="WORD: Correct: (remove 'in your group') | The phrase repeats information already given ('your group of designers ... in your group'). It's redundant; delete the trailing 'in your group' to avoid repetition.">in your group</mark>.

Speaker B: Okay. I'm Andre. Every time when you say I'm Roman, it's a bit strange. You're not Roman, you're not Romanian, you're Roman. Okay. <mark class="grammar-error" title="ORDER: Correct: Yesterday there was a pilot meeting for my English-speaking club. | Existential sentence needs 'there' and 'club' is more natural singular; 'English-speaking' is a compound adjective.">Yesterday was a pilot meeting of my English speaking clubs.</mark> And. <mark class="grammar-error" title="VERB: Correct: There were six people, including Kate and me. | Use existential 'there' and plural verb 'were' with a plural subject; pronoun order 'Kate and me' is natural in object position.">It was six people including me and Kate.</mark> <mark class="grammar-error" title="ARTICLE: Correct: They were from the design industry, or almost from it. | Use the definite article 'the' with 'design industry' and add 'from it' to avoid repeating the noun.">They were from design industry or almost design industry.</mark> So one girl from UX writing and three designers, <mark class="grammar-error" title="COLLOC: Correct: different levels, from senior designers to the head of design. | Unnatural collocation and structure: use 'from ... to ...' and specify 'senior designers' and 'head of design'.">different levels like seniors to design head.</mark> <mark class="grammar-error" title="ARTICLE: Correct: I started the meeting at 9 pm. | Missing article 'the' before 'meeting'.">I started meeting at 9pm</mark> so they were working the whole day and then we were speaking. annotation_items.explanation: When talking about someone's experience, English typically uses the preposition 'about' after 'hear' (hear about your experience), otherwise the phrase sounds incomplete or ungrammatical.
The phrase repeats information already given ('your group of designers ... in your group'). It's redundant; delete the trailing 'in your group' to avoid repetition.

annotation_items.text: Yesterday was a pilot meeting of my English speaking clubs.
annotation_items.correction: Yesterday there was a pilot meeting for my English-speaking club.
annotation_items.explanation: Existential sentence needs 'there' and 'club' is more natural singular; 'English-speaking' is a compound adjective.

annotation_items.text: It was six people including me and Kate.
annotation_items.correction: There were six people, including Kate and me.
annotation_items.explanation: Use existential 'there' and plural verb 'were' with a plural subject; pronoun order 'Kate and me' is natural in object position.

annotation_items.text: They were from design industry or almost design industry.
annotation_items.correction: They were from the design industry, or almost from it.
annotation_items.explanation: Use the definite article 'the' with 'design industry' and add 'from it' to avoid repeating the noun.

annotation_items.text: different levels like seniors to design head.
annotation_items.correction: different levels, from senior designers to the head of design.
annotation_items.explanation: Unnatural collocation and structure: use 'from ... to ...' and specify 'senior designers' and 'head of design'.

annotation_items.correction: I started the meeting at 9 pm.
annotation_items.explanation: Missing article 'the' before 'meeting'.

annotation_items.text: not too fast as I wanted
annotation_items.correction: not as fast as I wanted
annotation_items.explanation: Adjective comparison order should be 'not as X as', not 'not X as'.
Transitive preposition 'for' needs an object here; with no object use 'prepare' alone.

annotation_items.text: Was hard a bit to facilitate this meeting
annotation_items.correction: It was a bit hard to facilitate this meeting.
annotation_items.explanation: Missing subject 'It' and natural adverb order is 'a bit hard', not 'hard a bit'.

annotation_items.correction: the meaning of what they said
annotation_items.explanation: English uses 'meaning' or 'sense' with 'of/what they said' rather than 'sense from them'.

annotation_items.correction: ask questions in response to their replies
annotation_items.explanation: Wrong preposition/structure; 'for their replies' is not the usual way to say 'to get a reply' or 'in response to'.
Narration is in the past; use past tense 'replied' for consistency.

annotation_items.text: But the good things I can say that I tried to.
annotation_items.correction: But the good thing I can say is that I tried to.
annotation_items.explanation: Singular 'thing' fits the clause; the sentence needs linking verb 'is' and correct word order.

annotation_items.text: To involve people for the conversation
annotation_items.correction: to involve people in the conversation
annotation_items.explanation: Use 'in' with 'involve people in the conversation' (not 'for').

annotation_items.text: when I saw someone is silent or too quiet.
annotation_items.correction: when I saw someone was silent or too quiet.
annotation_items.explanation: Sequence of past events requires past tense 'was' after 'saw'.

annotation_items.text: picked someone to answer for my question.
annotation_items.correction: picked someone to answer my question.
annotation_items.explanation: Do not use 'answer for' here; use 'answer (my question)' or 'answer for' only in different contexts.

annotation_items.text: Also I get some feedback from people.
annotation_items.correction: Also I got some feedback from people.
annotation_items.explanation: Past event should use past tense 'got' rather than present 'get'.

annotation_items.text: I asked the. Honest, honest feedback
annotation_items.explanation: Missing preposition 'for' after 'asked' when requesting something; repetition 'honest, honest' is disfluent.
'Things' is vague; 'feedback' or 'comments' is the appropriate collocation here.
Duplicate definite article: only one 'the' is needed (or use 'all the feedback').
'of' is unnecessary here; say 'from all participants' or 'from all of the participants'.
Unnatural collocation: 'with complexity' is not standard; use 'in depth' or 'thoroughly'.

annotation_items.text: Acquire some people from my channel
annotation_items.correction: invite some people from my channel to the next meetings
annotation_items.explanation: Wrong collocation/word choice: we don't 'acquire' people; use 'invite' or 'recruit' and include destination ('to the next meetings').

annotation_items.text: Is it. Good amount of people
annotation_items.correction: Was it a good amount of people
annotation_items.explanation: Tense and article: for a past event use past tense 'Was' and include article 'a' before 'good amount'.
Tense/structure: referring to a past meeting, use past 'there were' (or 'it was six people').

annotation_items.correction: make it shorter / shorten it to X minutes
annotation_items.explanation: Unnatural phrasing: 'think shorter for minutes' is incorrect; use 'make it shorter' or 'shorten it to [minutes]'.

annotation_items.text: from your last paragraph from beginning
annotation_items.correction: from the beginning of your last paragraph
annotation_items.explanation: Wrong word order and missing article: say 'from the beginning of your last paragraph' (not 'from ... from beginning').
Wrong preposition: use 'answer to' (or 'answer your question'), not 'answer for'.

annotation_items.correction: I will repeat it for you.
annotation_items.explanation: Missing object pronoun: include 'it' (or 'that') after 'repeat' in this context.
Unnatural collocation/word choice: 'speak straightforward to' is not standard; use 'speak directly into' or 'speak straight into'.

annotation_items.text: do not take like this one
annotation_items.correction: do not hold it like this
annotation_items.explanation: Wrong lexical verb and structure: 'take' is incorrect here; use 'hold it like this' to describe microphone handling.

annotation_items.text: I just try to find some words on my wall.
annotation_items.correction: I was just trying to find some words on my wall.
annotation_items.explanation: Tense/aspect: use past continuous ('was just trying') to describe a recent ongoing action that was interrupted.
Native speakers say 'shorten the meeting' or 'reduce the meeting length' rather than 'decrease minutes'. This is an unnatural collocation.

annotation_items.correction: invite four people to a 40-minute meeting
annotation_items.explanation: Wrong preposition/structure: you 'invite someone to a meeting' and specify duration as 'a 40-minute meeting'.

annotation_items.text: In this way meeting will be more dynamic
annotation_items.correction: In this way, the meeting will be more dynamic
annotation_items.explanation: The noun 'meeting' needs a determiner here: 'the meeting'.
'Dynamical' is not the usual adjective in this context; use 'dynamic'.

annotation_items.text: do you have any strategy on this project
annotation_items.correction: Do you have any strategy for this project?
annotation_items.explanation: Question word order is incorrect and the preposition is wrong; use 'Do you have...?' and 'strategy for' a project.
Use 'in six months' or 'over six months' to indicate a time period; 'during' is not natural here.

annotation_items.text: for the growing up to grow up
annotation_items.correction: for the project to grow / for growth to happen
annotation_items.explanation: The phrase is nonsensical in English; use 'for the project to grow' or 'for growth to happen'.

annotation_items.text: the same way to acquire people as first 50 users
annotation_items.correction: the same way I used to acquire the first 50 users
annotation_items.explanation: The clause is missing structure and articles. A clearer form is 'the same way I used to acquire the first 50 users.'
Missing indefinite article 'an' before a singular countable noun 'individual service'.
The phrase needs an article and a natural preposition: 'participants in the club' or 'participants of the club.'
Wrong word order for a question in English; need auxiliary 'did' and subject-verb inversion.

annotation_items.text: That you are going to acquire 15 or 50
annotation_items.correction: That you are going to acquire 15 or 50 users
annotation_items.explanation: Missing the noun that identifies what '15 or 50' refers to (e.g. 'users').

annotation_items.correction: over six months / in six months
annotation_items.explanation: Use 'in/over six months' rather than 'during' when indicating a time period in this context.

annotation_items.text: mock interviews from for with the recruiter
annotation_items.explanation: Extra/misplaced prepositions ('from', 'for') — correct preposition is 'with'.
Missing definite article 'the' before a specific role ('design lead').
'Another' requires a singular noun; use 'another segment' (or 'other segments').
'Others' is incorrect here; use the adjective 'other' to modify 'potential segments'.
Word order is wrong: the object should come before the time phrase for natural English.
Missing definite article 'the' after 'one of' when referring to a specific group of schools.
Wrong pronoun 'your' — should be 'you' (or omit pronoun and say 'paying monthly').
Direct question order requires inversion: 'how should it work?'.
Missing indefinite article 'a' before 'subscription' (or use 'a subscription model').

annotation_items.text: I don't have brother at all.
annotation_items.correction: I don't have a brother at all.
annotation_items.explanation: Missing indefinite article 'a' before the singular countable noun 'brother'.
Missing definite article before 'session' in this context ('the session').
Wrong word order/choice; English uses 'something else' (or 'something like that'), not 'something like else'.
Incomplete question — missing the pronoun/object ('it').
Incorrect preposition and missing article: use 'under the pillow'.
Wrong verb form after 'start' — use the gerund ('acquiring') or 'to acquire'.

annotation_items.text: call after two weeks with users
annotation_items.explanation: Incorrect word order; in English put the object ('users') before the time phrase or say 'call the users after two weeks'.
Extra/misplaced word 'became' — remove it so the clause reads 'why they came to the club'.
Incorrect verb form — use the past simple form 'expected' in this reported clause, not 'did expect'.

annotation_items.text: is it enough now or they need something else?
annotation_items.correction: is it enough now, or do they need something else?
annotation_items.explanation: Question structure / word order error — the second clause needs the auxiliary 'do' for a question ('do they need').
Collocation: the idiomatic phrase is 'for a start' (or 'to start'), not 'for the start'.
Wrong preposition: English does not use 'join to' with a person/organization — say 'join you' or 'come to you'.
Missing article 'the' before 'option' — correct phrase is 'would like the option to...'.

annotation_items.text: say find me in the club.
annotation_items.correction: for example, find me in the club.
annotation_items.explanation: The discourse marker 'say' is misused here; use 'for example' or 'say,' with commas: '...the option to, say, find me in the club.'

annotation_items.text: share feedback from your students to me?
annotation_items.correction: share feedback from your students with me?
annotation_items.explanation: Wrong preposition after 'share' — feedback is shared 'with' someone, not 'to' them.
'live speaking' is an awkward collocation; reorder or rephrase as 'speaking live' or 'live practice and speaking'.
'hardness' is not the natural collocation in this context; 'difficulty level' is the standard phrase.
word order/word choice is incorrect; say 'level of difficulty' to refer to how hard something is.

annotation_items.correction: just a hard topic for them
annotation_items.explanation: missing indefinite article before a singular countable noun ('a hard topic').

annotation_items.text: discuss on this topic about dark patterns
annotation_items.correction: discuss this topic in English (dark patterns and empathy)
annotation_items.explanation: incorrect preposition use and redundant 'about'; the verb 'discuss' takes a direct object ('discuss this topic') and specify the language with 'in English'.
double comparative ('more harder') is incorrect; use either 'harder' or 'more difficult'.
wrong subject-verb structure: use 'There were' to introduce existence of plural 'moments'.

annotation_items.correction: it was a meeting without a presentation
annotation_items.explanation: missing articles before singular nouns ('a meeting', 'a presentation').
missing article before the noun phrase; also 'ping-pong format' is a fixed collocation that commonly takes an article.

annotation_items.text: It was not lesson on my meeting
annotation_items.correction: It was not a lesson at my meeting
annotation_items.explanation: missing article before 'lesson' and wrong preposition; say 'a lesson' and use 'at/in my meeting'—'at my meeting' is natural here.
in a short negative response the subject and auxiliary are needed ('It's not'); standalone 'Is not' is ungrammatical.

annotation_items.text: positive things that I'm a good facilitator
annotation_items.correction: a positive thing is that I'm a good facilitator
annotation_items.explanation: sentence structure is incomplete/misordered; introduce the idea with 'A positive thing is that...' or 'A positive is that...'.
unnecessary preposition 'in' before 'last year'—use 'last year' without 'in'.
'Both' needs a complement (e.g. 'both are possible' or 'both options'). The utterance is incomplete as spoken.
Singular count noun requires an indefinite article ('an AI assistant').

annotation_items.text: Will be fully comparable with us?
annotation_items.correction: Will it be fully comparable to us?
annotation_items.explanation: Question is missing a subject ('it') and 'comparable to' is the usual preposition for comparisons with people.

annotation_items.correction: you don't even see the difference
annotation_items.explanation: Standard negative word order places 'even' after the auxiliary ('don't even'), and 'difference' requires the definite article 'the'.

annotation_items.correction: a human voice and a model's voice
annotation_items.explanation: With countable nouns here, articles are expected ('a human voice', 'a model's voice') to make the comparison natural.

annotation_items.text: So how you evaluate the timing
annotation_items.correction: So how do you evaluate the timing
annotation_items.explanation: Direct questions in English require auxiliary inversion ('do you evaluate'). The auxiliary 'do' is missing.

annotation_items.correction: such models (or this kind of model)
annotation_items.explanation: 'Such kind of' is a non-standard collocation; use 'such models' or 'this kind of model'.
The verb 'join' is transitive here and does not take the preposition 'to' before the noun ('join your meeting' or 'join the meeting').

annotation_items.text: this type of meeting more about is more about
annotation_items.correction: this type of meeting is more about
annotation_items.explanation: Word order is broken and the verb 'is' is misplaced; the correct structure is 'this type of meeting is more about ...'.
The intensive adverb 'too' (meaning excessive) is required here; 'to' is the incorrect preposition/word.
When referring to a specific era, English requires the definite article 'the' ('the AI era').

annotation_items.text: I'm not sure that I catch the main thought
annotation_items.correction: I'm not sure that I caught the main thought from your speech
annotation_items.explanation: The speaker refers to a past reception of the speech; the past tense 'caught' is required instead of 'catch'.
Missing indefinite article 'a' before a singular count noun.

annotation_items.text: no real experience from AI model
annotation_items.correction: no real experience from an AI model
annotation_items.explanation: A singular countable noun ('AI model') requires an article ('an').
After 'provide' when indicating recipient and object, English uses 'provide somebody with something' (or 'provide something to somebody'); the original lacks 'with' and misorders nouns.
Before a singular countable noun (expert) an indefinite article is required: 'an expert'.

annotation_items.text: writing with AI for instance any themes regarding health
annotation_items.correction: writing with AI about health topics
annotation_items.explanation: The phrase needs a preposition ('about'/'on') to link 'writing with AI' to the topics; 'themes' is also awkward—'health topics' is the natural phrasing.
'Deep learning' is a technical term for a method, so here the intended meaning is likely 'deep knowledge'; the collocation 'has very deep learning' is unnatural.

annotation_items.text: interesting for me having it in speaking
annotation_items.correction: interesting for me to have it for speaking
annotation_items.explanation: After 'interesting for me' use the infinitive ('to have') rather than the gerund; also 'for speaking' is a more natural prepositional phrase than 'in speaking'.

annotation_items.text: expert in for instance in some details
annotation_items.correction: expert, for instance, in some details
annotation_items.explanation: Word order is disrupted by the inserted 'for instance' and an extra 'in'; move 'for instance' and remove the redundant preposition to form a correct clause.

annotation_items.correction: a bit of a specific industry
annotation_items.explanation: Missing article/structure before the countable noun 'industry'. Use 'a bit of a ...' or 'a somewhat ...'.
Missing possessive marker 's' after 'someone else' to show possession.
Missing definite article 'the' before 'same cases'.

annotation_items.text: I and Gen GPT wrote your negative feedback
annotation_items.correction: Gen GPT and I wrote your negative feedback
annotation_items.explanation: Non-standard word order: put the other person(s) before 'I' in subject coordination.
Incorrect/omitted pronoun; 'some' + adjective requires a noun or use 'something'.

annotation_items.text: I don't want to only speak
annotation_items.correction: I don't want to be the only one speaking
annotation_items.explanation: Unnatural/incomplete structure for expressing 'not the only speaker' — need 'be the only one speaking'.
Non-parallel and incorrect number: use plural 'dark patterns' (or 'a dark pattern') to match the general concept.
Missing auxiliary and wrong word order — needs 'it's' (it is) and a clearer structure.

annotation_items.text: your decrease in expenditure on marketing
annotation_items.explanation: Use a verb after the subject: 'expenditure ... decreases' (or rephrase 'if you decrease your marketing expenditure').

annotation_items.text: your decrease in customer loyalty or how to say so only 1%
annotation_items.correction: your decrease in customer loyalty is only 1%
annotation_items.explanation: Missing copula 'is' before 'only 1%'.
'such kind of' is non-standard collocation; use 'this kind of' or 'such' + noun.
Missing preposition 'on' (or wrong word order).
Subject–verb agreement: singular 'NPS' requires 'decreases'.

annotation_items.text: I'm not sure that I have an answer that it's a red line
annotation_items.correction: I'm not sure I can say it's a strict red line / I'm not sure whether it's a strict red line
annotation_items.explanation: Broken sentence structure and awkward clause linking; needs rephrasing to express uncertainty properly.

annotation_items.text: It's always pros and cons of this decision
annotation_items.correction: There are always pros and cons to this decision
annotation_items.explanation: Incorrect sentence structure; English uses 'there are' and 'pros and cons' take 'to' (or 'for') with 'decision'.
The adjective 'evident' takes the preposition 'to' when indicating the perceiver.

annotation_items.text: for my budget that runs much better
annotation_items.correction: and my budget is running/performing much better
annotation_items.explanation: Unclear clause structure; 'budget that runs much better' is ungrammatical — use a finite verb ('is running/performing').

annotation_items.text: or we have three minutes left
annotation_items.correction: or do we have three minutes left
annotation_items.explanation: In a question with two alternatives the second clause needs the auxiliary 'do' to form a question ('or do we...').

annotation_items.text: what do you think the most common mistakes for you
annotation_items.correction: what do you think are the most common mistakes for you
annotation_items.explanation: Missing copula/auxiliary 'are' in the question; correct word order for a question about 'mistakes' requires 'are'.
Subject–verb agreement: 'he' requires 'has'.
Wrong word: speaker meant 'tenses' (verb forms), not 'times'.
Incomplete structure: 'correct' needs an object (e.g. 'them'/'it') to complete the idea.

annotation_items.text: the mistakes like this annoying, annoying
annotation_items.explanation: Missing copula 'are' (verb) and corrected word order for a full sentence.

annotation_items.text: Annoying and complicated thing for me for.
annotation_items.correction: An annoying and complicated thing for me.
annotation_items.explanation: Missing article 'an' before the noun; extra trailing 'for' is unnecessary.

annotation_items.text: I have two one for simplifying our process.
annotation_items.correction: I have two: one is for simplifying our process.
annotation_items.explanation: Run-on/fragment: needs a connector and verb ('one is') to link ideas clearly.
